How transcription factor binding controls transcriptional bursting dynamics: A single-molecule view Shasha Chong1,* and Shawn Yoshida1,2 1Division of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA 2Division of Biology and Biological Engineering, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA *Correspondence: schong@caltech.edu https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2024.02.027

In this issue, Pomp et al.1 simultaneously tracked transcriptional bursts of yeast gene GAL10 and transient binding of transcription factor Gal4 at the gene using novel methods. Dynamic exchange and infrequent long binding of Gal4 together enable prolonged transcriptional bursts of GAL10.

Transcriptional regulation is fundamental to all cellular functions. Sequence-specific DNA-binding transcription factors (TFs) are key players in eukaryotic transcriptional regulation, recruiting cofactors and the preinitiation complex to activate transcription. Single-molecule imaging has revealed that TF-DNA interactions are highly dynamic, with binding residence times in the range of seconds to tens of seconds.2 Transcription is also inherently dynamic and occurs in stochastic bursts, with burst durations reported from seconds to minutes to hours depending on the gene and organism under investigation.3,4 A fundamental outstanding question is how such transient, seconds-long TF binding robustly

activates transcription, where a burst can last minutes or even hours. Identifying the molecular mechanisms that connect the two timescales is critical to understanding transcriptional regulation, but a major challenge here was the lack of a method for simultaneously quantifying TF-binding and transcriptional-bursting dynamics at a single gene. Although TFbinding dynamics are routinely measured using live-cell single-molecule tracking2 and bursting transcription of a target gene can be visualized using well-established MS2 or PP7 labeling techniques,5 it has been technically challenging to measure TF binding at a single gene that is actively being transcribed, leaving understanding the dynamic interplay be-

tween the two processes infeasible. Pomp et al. developed a microscopy method to address the challenges. They chose the well-studied yeast TF Gal4 and its highly expressed target gene GAL10 as the model system. A key innovation here is the development of a tracking algorithm to keep the PP7labeled GAL10 transcription site in focus with an active feedback loop while only imaging a single plane in the z direction. This new tracking algorithm is compatible with single-molecule imaging of Gal4 and enables simultaneous measurements of its dynamic binding to the GAL10 locus and transcriptional bursts of GAL10. The authors then used this method to test three kinetic models that have been

Molecular Cell 84, March 21, 2024 a 2024 Elsevier Inc.

[Page 2]

Figure 1. Transcription factor cooperative binding and dynamic exchange enables long transcriptional bursts (A) Comparison of example time traces of transcriptional bursts of the gene GAL10 and the transcription factor (TF) Gal4’s binding to DNA described by three different models. The green trajectory represents GAL10 transcription, and the dashed magenta trajectory represents Gal4 binding/unbinding. (B) The long-residing subpopulation model and cooperative binding with exchange model both feature one rate-limiting step of gene activation. The multi-step activation model has multiple rate-limiting steps of gene activation. (C) Cartoon illustrating the TF cooperative binding with exchange model. GAL10 on and off times can be extracted from the trajectories of its bursting transcription. (D) Summary of evidence that supports each of the three models. Only 16% of GAL10 bursts completely overlapped with a Gal4 binding event, suggesting that the long-residing subpopulation of Gal4 plays a relatively minor role in GAL10 bursting and that cooperative binding with exchange of Gal4 is the dominant driver of the bursting dynamics. Frequency of association and dissociation events during transcriptional bursts support the model of cooperative binding with exchange but do not rule out the contribution of the long-residing TF subpopulation.

proposed to explain the disconnect between the dynamics of TF binding and transcription, i.e., (1) the long-residing subpopulation model, where a subpopulation of TFs bind DNA for long residence times that determine the duration of bursts, though the majority of TFs bind much more transiently; (2) the multi-step activation model, where TF binding initiates many subsequent steps that activate tran-

scription even after TF dissociation; and (3) the cooperative binding with exchange model, where DNA-bound TFs increase the binding rate of subsequent TFs and where TFs, each having short residence times, constantly exchange throughout the duration of bursts (Figures 1A–1C).4,6,7 The authors acquired fluorescence intensity traces over time of both Gal4 binding and GAL10 transcription in many live

yeast cells using different Gal4 labeling densities and imaging speeds. Cross-correlation and trace alignment analyses of the Gal4-GAL10 time traces revealed that Gal4 binding precedes GAL10 transcription, consistent with previously reported findings.8 More importantly, Gal4 association has an almost immediate activating effect on transcription, and Gal4 dissociation coincides with a decrease in transcription. In other words, Gal4 association acts on transcription rapidly (within a few seconds) and determines the start and end of transcription bursts. These findings are in line with models 1 and 3 but not with model 2, which instead predicts that TF dissociation is not followed by an immediate decrease in transcription. This result is also consistent with the observed distribution of the GAL10 off periods, which suggests that there is a single rate-limiting step (described in models 1 and 3) instead of multiple rate-limiting steps (described in model 2) to turn on GAL10 transcription. Further analysis of the Gal4-GAL10 time traces suggests that 20% of all the detected Gal4 binding events result in activation of GAL10 transcription. The authors further determined the association and dissociation frequency of Gal4 in different periods relative to transcriptional bursts. They found that during a burst, the Gal4 association frequency is significantly higher than before or after a burst and equally as high as at the start of a burst. Similarly, Gal4 dissociation frequency is high during a burst and at the end of a burst. The observation of high Gal4 association and dissociation frequencies during a burst supports the TF exchange model (model 3). On the other hand, using experimental settings that reduce photobleaching in tracking fluorescently labeled Gal4, the authors captured a previously undetected long-binding subpopulation of Gal4 with residence times on the same order of magnitude as the duration of transcriptional bursts. Although only 16% of the detected transcriptional bursts overlap with a single long Gal4-binding event, these results support the longresiding subpopulation model (model 1) (Figure 1D). In short, using new powerful imaging methods that simultaneously measure the dynamics of Gal4 binding and transcriptional bursting at GAL10, the authors revealed that cooperative binding and

[Page 3]

dynamic exchange of Gal4 at the GAL10 promoter is the dominant mechanism by which Gal4 enables prolonged transcriptional bursts of GAL10. A subpopulation of Gal4 remains bound during the entire burst. These long-binding events, though infrequent, also contribute to maintaining long transcriptional bursts. It is noteworthy that instead of independently binding, Gal4 molecules bind to multiple target sequences in the GAL10 promoter cooperatively, as indicated by the temporally clustered binding events during a transcriptional burst. A possible mechanism underlying cooperative binding of Gal4 is its homotypic interactions9 that enable recruitment of additional Gal4 molecules to sites where Gal4 is already bound. Since an increasing number of TFs are reported to have homotypic interaction capabilities10 and promoters and enhancers usually contain multiple TF binding sites, cooperative binding and exchange of TFs may be a general kinetic mode applicable to the activation of genes besides GAL10 in yeast. This model may also include cooperation of different TFs that have heterotypic TF-TF interactions.
